title: ASML Eyes Higher Lithography Tool Prices as TSMC Pushes Back, Highlighting Growing Cost Tensions in Chip Manufacturing
gist: ASML's EUV production capacity is now nearly fully booked through end-2027, giving it pricing confidence even as TSMC resists proposed price increases and remains cautious on High-NA EUV (~$400M/tool), while several Chinese customers have already accepted roughly 10% DUV price increases; TSMC reportedly does not expect to adopt High-NA EUV even for its A13 process planned around 2029.

themes.theme: ASML's booked-out capacity underpins pricing power

insights.claim: ASML's EUV production capacity is now nearly fully booked through the end of 2027, which CFO Roger Dassen says has strengthened the company's pricing position, with discussions on higher pricing already underway with customers.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: A multi-year (through 2027) EUV capacity backlog is a hard confirmation of ASML's near-monopoly pricing leverage, directly relevant to modeling ASML's forward ASP and gross margin trajectory.
insights.quote: The company's EUV production capacity is now nearly fully booked through the end of 2027, giving ASML greater confidence in its long-term pricing strategy.

insights.claim: Several Chinese customers have already accepted price increases of around 10% for ASML's mature DUV lithography systems, reflecting continued strong demand for DUV equipment used in mature-node production as domestic Chinese semiconductor manufacturing capacity expands.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: A concrete ~10% DUV price increase already accepted by Chinese buyers is a quantified data point for ASML's China-driven DUV revenue/margin upside, distinct from the more contentious EUV pricing negotiations with TSMC.
insights.quote: Reports indicate that several Chinese customers have already accepted price increases of around 10% for ASML's mature deep ultraviolet (DUV) lithography systems.

insights.claim: TSMC has remained cautious about adopting ASML's High-NA EUV systems, which cost approximately $400 million per tool, and industry reports suggest TSMC does not expect to introduce the technology even for its A13 manufacturing process, currently planned around 2029.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: A specific claim that TSMC may skip High-NA EUV through at least its A13 node (~2029) is a significant divergence from Intel's and Samsung's earlier High-NA adoption plans, with direct implications for ASML's highest-value tool segment revenue mix and for competitive lithography strategy comparisons across foundries.
insights.quote: Industry reports suggest the company does not expect to introduce the technology even for its A13 manufacturing process, currently planned around 2029.

insights.claim: ASML raised its 2026 revenue guidance for the second time this year and announced plans to expand EUV production capacity by roughly 30% annually over the next two years while increasing DUV output to support customer demand across multiple technology nodes.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Confirms (from an independent source) the same ~30% annual EUV capacity expansion plan reported in ASML's own earnings call, reinforcing the reliability of that capacity-growth signal as an input for WFE supply-chain and foundry-capex modeling.
insights.quote: The company also announced plans to expand EUV production capacity by roughly 30% annually over the next two years while increasing output of DUV systems to support customer demand across multiple technology nodes.
